title: WayfinderAI
tagline: AI-powered indoor navigation — no GPS, just vision
description: Two independent works under the same project — not yet integrated, but sharing the same goal: indoor navigation without GPS, beacons, or hardware.

parts.label: Part 1 — Local VLM Pipeline
parts.summary: Fine-tuned Qwen3-VL-8B to convert raw floorplan images into structured navigation graphs — tackling the hardest input problem: any arbitrary floor plan, any format.
bullets: Input: hand-drawn sketches, scanned PDFs, or CAD exports
Output: structured navigation graph JSON ready for pathfinding
GRPO's reward-based signal produced valid graph JSON far more reliably than supervised fine-tuning alone

parts.label: Part 2 — Kroger Grocery Navigation App
parts.summary: Voice-first, node-based navigation for Kroger stores. The Kroger Developer API exposes aisle-level product locations for 300,000+ products — WayfinderAI converts this into a live navigation graph. Won 1st Place at Hack@URI AI Track (30+ teams).
bullets: 5-agent pipeline: store graph builder → LLM chatbot → route optimizer → OCR aisle reader → shelf scanner
Dijkstra vs Nearest-Neighbor dual routing — always picks the faster path
EasyOCR reads physical aisle signs (A1–A42) to confirm real-world location mid-route
YOLO-World detects any of 300,000+ Kroger products on shelf without retraining

highlights: Part 1 — Local VLM: fine-tuned Qwen3-VL-8B via LoRA + GRPO for floorplan-to-navigation-graph generation
Part 2 — Kroger app: 5-agent pipeline converts Kroger API data into a live navigation graph in <10s
Dijkstra vs Nearest-Neighbor dual routing — always picks the faster path
EasyOCR reads physical aisle signs to confirm real-world location mid-route
YOLO-World detects any of 300,000+ products on shelf without retraining
Shipped live Vercel demo within 8 weeks
challenges: The two hardest problems are at opposite ends: Part 1 deals with wildly inconsistent floorplan formats (hand-drawn, scanned, CAD) — solved with progressive fine-tuning. Part 2 deals with real-world location confirmation without GPS — solved with EasyOCR reading physical aisle signs and a Claude Vision fallback.
learnings: Splitting a problem into independent parallel tracks is underrated. Part 1 and Part 2 were built separately, which meant neither blocked the other. The integration challenge comes later — but both are stronger for having been developed independently first.

title: Apex AI
tagline: Vision-only AI race engineer — no sensors, just video
description: AI race engineer that watches your onboard karting or motorcycle lap and delivers structured coaching — timestamped mistakes, best moments, performance scores, and lap time loss estimates. Built in two separate versions.
inspiration: I got the initial idea at the Vision Hackathon. Most teams were focused on executing existing ideas and hoping their implementation would stand out — I took a different approach and worked on something I was genuinely passionate about: Formula 1. I've always been drawn to finding ways to minimize the cost of sensors and heavy data processing in motorsport. That's where Apex/PitLane AI came from — upload an onboard lap video and get back structured race engineering insights. What race engineers do by analyzing sensor telemetry, this does from video alone.

parts.label: V1 — PitLane AI (Twelve Labs)
parts.summary: Powered by two Twelve Labs models — Pegasus for whole-video semantic analysis and Marengo for video search. Won the Twelve Labs Computer Vision Hackathon at Northeastern University.
bullets: Pegasus watches the full lap and finds errors across 4 categories: Racing Line, Braking, Throttle & Traction, Car Control
Find Best Moments across 6 categories — smooth apex, clean acceleration, perfect racing line, and more
Ask free-form questions: 'Where am I losing the most time?' — returns verdict + timestamped moments
Coaching report: performance scores (1–10), sector breakdown, lap time loss in tenths of seconds, top 3 fixes
Two surfaces: FiftyOne plugin (run operators on video dataset) + React web app

parts.label: V2 — Apex AI (Fully Local)
parts.futurePlans: Experiment further with modularity — local models feel like the right direction here. The pipeline is computationally heavy, and rather than building a general-purpose system, a more expert-driven approach would be stronger: purpose-built pipelines that only activate models relevant to the specific application, and deliver results shaped around what that sport or discipline actually demands.
parts.summary: I wanted to avoid relying on paid API models and instead explore how far a fully local, modular pipeline could go — breaking the task into small pieces each handled by a lightweight local model. Supports karting, biking, and cycling from a single onboard video.
bullets: Stage 1: CV events loader — YOLOv8n + Farneback optical flow extracts braking, racing line, and speed delta events with severity scores
Stage 2: Florence-2-base (232M params, CPU) triages each keyframe — vehicle_visible, in_corner, obstacle_ahead via <CAPTION> + <OD> tasks
Stage 3: Conditional llava reasoning via Ollama — only fires on significant events or obstacle frames, skips the rest
Stage 4: Parallel llama3.2 coaching note writer — asyncio + ThreadPoolExecutor (4 workers) generates per-event coaching notes concurrently
Stage 5: llama3.2 session aggregator synthesises all notes into a final structured coaching report
CV-only fallback mode when Ollama is unavailable — no hard API dependency

challenges: V1: Twelve Labs returns rich but unstructured observations — getting Groq LLaMA to extract valid JSON performance scores without hallucinating required careful prompt engineering and retry logic. V2: Florence-2's <OD> task on CPU is ~1–3s per frame — running it on every frame would take hours. The fix was a two-gate system: only triage keyframes flagged by the CV events loader, then only invoke llava on frames that pass Florence-2's severity threshold.
learnings: The API approach (V1) is undeniably more reliable — Twelve Labs Pegasus produces richer, more accurate coaching than anything a local pipeline can match today. But the modular local approach (V2) showed that dividing the problem into small focused tasks — each handled by the right lightweight model — gets you surprisingly far without spending a dollar. The two versions are not competing; they represent two different constraints, and both are worth knowing.

title: Edge AI Multisensor System
tagline: Distributed edge intelligence for building automation with multi-sensor pipelines
description: A multi-hub edge AI system that runs 5 parallel edge hub threads — each monitoring a building zone via real BMS sensor data (VAVs, RTUs) — and a central coordinator that escalates anomalies. All AI runs locally via Ollama. No cloud, no latency, no data leaving the building.
longDescription: Each edge hub polls live VAV (variable air volume) and RTU (rooftop unit) sensor readings, computes divergence scores against setpoints, and classifies anomalies as moderate or critical using an active inference framework. Critical events are escalated to a CVC (central validation coordinator) running llama3.2 locally. The system supports 8 named fault scenarios — from single zone overheat to a full multi-hub crisis — and sends alerts via Telegram to engineers and operators.
highlights: 5 parallel edge hub threads, each monitoring a dedicated building zone independently
Real BMS data: 26 VAVs + 4 RTUs across 5 zones — real sensor readings, not mocked
Active inference anomaly detection — divergence score triggers moderate/critical classification
Ollama llama3.2 runs entirely locally — no cloud API calls, no data leaves the building
8 named fault scenarios: zone overheat, sensor loss, power surge, RTU fault, multi-hub crisis
Telegram alerting to separate engineer and operator channels on critical events
challenges: Designing a two-level model hierarchy (hub LLM + CVC LLM) where each hub acts independently but the coordinator synthesises across all zones required careful message passing and timeout handling — if a hub is slow, the CVC can't block waiting for it.
learnings: Edge AI for physical infrastructure is fundamentally different from cloud AI — latency, reliability, and data residency constraints dominate design decisions more than raw accuracy. Running llama3.2 locally was non-negotiable for a real building deployment.

title: Intelligent Follow-Me Robot
tagline: Real-time person-following robot using phone as camera and IMU
description: A person-following robot pipeline that uses an Android phone as the only sensor — streaming video and IMU data over WiFi. The laptop runs YOLOv8 + ReID + KCF tracking and sends BLE motor commands to an Elegoo UNO R4 robot in real time.
longDescription: The pipeline runs entirely on a laptop with no GPU required. Each frame goes through YOLOv8 detection, zone-based ReID enrollment (head/upper/lower body embeddings + colour histograms + gait), KCF tracking between YOLO frames, Kalman smoothing, IMU-based shake detection, obstacle heuristics from bottom-zone pixel analysis, and a safety governor — all before a BLE command is sent to the robot. The system recovers from losing the target using gyro-integrated search sweeps.
highlights: Android phone as the only sensor — MJPEG video + gyro/accelerometer over WiFi
Zone-based ReID: head, upper body, lower body embeddings + colour histograms + gait signature
KCF tracker + Kalman filter between YOLO frames — smooth tracking at ~30Hz
IMU fusion: shake detection skips ReID on blurred frames, gyro gates IoU re-init on slopes
5-stage safety pipeline: obstacle heuristics → reflex layer → safety governor → BLE execution
Lost-person recovery using gyro-integrated directional search sweeps
challenges: The KCF tracker drifts onto walls when the target is lost — the fix was a hard YOLO veto rule: if YOLO runs and finds zero people, the tracker output is discarded entirely. No tracker is allowed to override a confirmed negative detection.
learnings: Multi-modal sensor fusion (vision + IMU) is where robotics gets interesting. The IMU doesn't track the person — it tracks the robot itself. Using it to detect when the robot is shaking (skip ReID) or turning fast (gate tracker re-init) made the system dramatically more robust.

title: Star Tracker CNN
tagline: Comparing U-Net variants for star detection in CubeSat star trackers
description: Research project benchmarking three U-Net architectures for star detection and centroid localisation on real DSS2 sky images — targeting CubeSat star tracker constraints where model size, inference time, and accuracy all matter equally.
longDescription: Part of the Northeastern Satellite Lab CubeSat Star Tracker project. Trained and evaluated UNet (481K params), MobileUNet (255K params), and ELUNet (59K params) on 250 real sky images from NASA SkyView's DSS2 Red Survey. Ground truth segmentation maps were generated by brightness thresholding and cross-referenced with the Gaia DR3 star catalog (1.8 billion stars). ELUNet — at 59K parameters — achieved F1 of 0.910 and 0.45px centroid error at 8.13ms inference, making it the strongest candidate for INT8 quantization on Raspberry Pi.
highlights: UNet: F1 0.928, centroid error 0.39px, 5.54ms inference — highest accuracy
MobileUNet: F1 0.884, centroid error 0.49px, 17.81ms — slowest despite fewer params
ELUNet: F1 0.910, centroid error 0.45px, 8.13ms — best size/speed/accuracy tradeoff at 59K params
250 real DSS2 Red Survey sky images, cross-referenced with Gaia DR3 star catalog
Targets CubeSat power constraints — model size and inference time are first-class constraints
challenges: MobileUNet — despite having fewer parameters than UNet — was the slowest model (17.81ms). The inverted residual blocks that make MobileNet efficient on classification tasks don't translate to segmentation in the same way, especially at this input resolution.
learnings: For satellite hardware, the right metric isn't F1 alone — it's F1 per millisecond per kilobyte of model size. ELUNet's aggressive channel reduction (base channels = 8) is what makes it quantization-friendly and the real candidate for deployment.
